note: Scored vs the VHS composite reference as a proxy (shared composite path). Scanlines + chroma bleed present; add antenna SNOW (random RF noise) and ghosting (multipath echo) which define off-air reception.
Cheap composite + OSD reads plausibly for public-access tape. The OSD label renders garbled ('CTFID CHANNEL3') — verify the OSD glyph/label set (recurring across several presets).
Composite + timestamp OSD plausible for an '84 cable-access recording (VHS proxy). Same garbled OSD label as Public Access.

Scored against an NTSC-composite reference as a proxy. PAL-specific traits are not distinct: finer 625-line scanlines, PAL's stable chroma (no NTSC hue error), and occasional Hanover bars. Needs a PAL reference.
UHF antenna reception needs prominent SNOW + ghosting; currently reads as clean-ish composite. PAL-proxy (NTSC ref); needs a PAL/UHF reference.

Does NOT look scrambled — reads as normal composite. Real analog cable scrambling (sync-suppression / gated-sync) produces a horizontally TORN/rolling/jittering picture, suppressed/inverted luma, and colour distortion. The defining scrambling artifact is missing.
Shows the Quadruplex 'venetian-blind' horizontal head-banding — the correct signature artifact of 2" quad. Strong. Consider a B&W default for a 1960s broadcast.
Plausible analog-capture-card look (composite into a PC capture). VHS-composite proxy; fine.
Correctly cleaner and sharper than VHS with only mild composite artifacts — right for LaserDisc's high-quality analog composite. Good differentiation from tape.

Betacam SP is broadcast-grade COMPONENT analog — should be markedly sharper/cleaner with better colour than the VHS-like composite look rendered here. Scored vs VHS proxy; needs a Betacam reference. OSD label garbled.
8mm Hi8 has higher luma bandwidth than VHS and a different colour-under behaviour; currently a VHS-generic composite look. Proxy-scored; needs a Hi8 reference.
8mm Video8 proxy-scored vs VHS; format-specific traits unverified. Camcorder OSD + composite look are plausible; needs a Video8 reference.

MiniDV is a DIGITAL (DV codec) format — should be clean and sharp with DV's intra-frame DCT texture on fine detail rather than analog scanlines. The OSD ('IMG_..._FINE 5M') reads as a still-camera, not a camcorder. Tighten toward DV behaviour.

Named 'Dropout-Prone' but the DV-specific dropout is not shown. DV error concealment produces SHARP rectangular block hits / frozen macroblocks / hold-last-good-block (often a single bright or mis-coloured DCT block), NOT analog horizontal streaks. Replace the analog-style dropout with DV block-error concealment.
HDV is MPEG-2 long-GOP on tape — should show MPEG-2 blocking/mosquito on motion and occasional GOP-drop stutter rather than an analog composite look. Re-anchor to the codec reference.
Correctly clean HD (D-VHS carries an MPEG-2 transport stream — pristine unless there are errors). Good restraint.

Good film-on-TV hybrid — film grain + TV scanlines + softness. Consider a stronger B&W default for a 1950s kinescope. The film-grain aspect should ultimately be confirmed against a real film reference (pending).
Severe, believable tape damage — heavy chroma bleed, warping, TBC OSD, dropouts. Inherits the dropout-shape caveat from advancedDropouts but the overall damaged read is convincing.
Reads as a cleaned-up transfer — reduced artifacts with residual grain/dust — which correctly represents archival restoration (surface noise reduced, not a pristine digital master).
